comments:
This paper provides a clean, substantial, and technically strong contribution to the theory of online learning with structured feedback. The authors give a complete minimax characterization (up to polylog factors) of the regret induced by arbitrary feedback graphs, identifying three regimes — strongly observable, weakly observable, and unobservable — with matching upper and lower bounds: Θ̃(α^{1/2} T^{1/2}), Θ̃(δ^{1/3} T^{2/3}), and Θ(T), respectively. The paper introduces the weak domination number δ as a graph parameter governing the hard T^{2/3} regime, presents a simple and efficient algorithm (Exp3.G) that attains the upper bounds, and supplies tight lower bounds. The technical development is solid: a refined second-order Hedge bound is proved and used effectively, and the reductions/relations to partial monitoring are clearly elucidated. The exposition is thorough and the proofs are detailed; lemmas and constructions (including time-varying graphs and the informed/uninformed distinction) strengthen the contribution. 

Minor suggestions: (1) Although this is primarily a theoretical paper, a short experimental section or illustrative simulations (even small-scale) could help convey practical implications of the three regimes to readers unfamiliar with these graph parameters. (2) The paper often focuses on binary losses for the partial-monitoring reduction and some lower bounds; an explicit discussion summarizing which results require binary losses and which extend to general [0,1] losses would aid clarity. (3) It would be helpful to include a bit more intuition and examples about the weak domination number (δ) and how it behaves on natural graph families. 

Overall, the manuscript is original, well-motivated, technically correct, and of high impact to the online learning community. I recommend acceptance and an oral presentation slot.
